section.title: The Ten Lepers and the Coming Kingdom
section.sectionRationale: Jesus warns against causing little ones to stumble and calls for endless forgiveness, then answers the plea for more faith by pointing to faith as small as a seed and to servants who claim no merit. On the road He heals ten lepers, but only one, a Samaritan, returns to give thanks. Asked when the kingdom will come, He says it is already in their midst, and He describes the sudden, unmistakable day of the Son of Man, which will come like lightning after He has first suffered and been rejected.
section.thesis: The kingdom is already present in the person of Jesus and will one day break in suddenly and universally, so its people are to forgive without limit, trust with humble faith, return in grateful worship, and lose their lives to keep them, ready for the day of the Son of Man.
section.frameNote: Four movements: stumbling blocks and unlimited forgiveness; faith like a seed and unworthy servants; the ten lepers and the grateful Samaritan; and the coming of the kingdom and the day of the Son of Man.

verses.text: Jesus said to His disciples, “It is inevitable that {{skandalon|stumbling blocks}} will come, but woe to the one through whom they come!
It would be better for him to have a millstone hung around his neck and to be thrown into the sea than to cause one of these little ones to stumble.
Watch yourselves. If your brother sins, rebuke him; and if he repents, forgive him.
Even if he sins against you seven times in a day, and seven times returns to say, ‘I repent,’ you must forgive him.”

units.label: Faith like a seed and unworthy servants
units.note: we have only done our duty

verses.text: The apostles said to the Lord, “Increase our {{pistis|faith}}!”
And the Lord answered, “If you have faith the size of a mustard seed, you can say to this mulberry tree, ‘Be uprooted and planted in the sea,’ and it will obey you.
Which of you whose servant comes in from plowing or shepherding in the field will say to him, ‘Come at once and sit down to eat’?
Instead, won’t he tell him, ‘Prepare my meal and dress yourself to serve me while I eat and drink, and afterward you may eat and drink’?
Does he thank the servant because he did what he was told?
So you also, when you have done everything commanded of you, should say, ‘We are unworthy servants; we have only done our duty.’”

units.label: The ten lepers and the grateful Samaritan
units.note: was no one found to return except this foreigner

verses.text: While Jesus was on His way to Jerusalem, He was passing between Samaria and Galilee.
As He entered one of the villages, He was met by ten lepers. They stood at a distance
and raised their voices, shouting, “Jesus, Master, have mercy on us!”
When Jesus saw them, He said, “Go, show yourselves to the priests.” And as they were on their way, they were cleansed.
When one of them saw that he was healed, he came back, praising God in a loud voice.
He fell facedown at Jesus’ feet in thanksgiving to Him, and he was a Samaritan.
“Were not all ten cleansed?” Jesus asked. “Where then are the other nine?
Was no one found except this foreigner to return and give glory to God?”
Then Jesus said to him, “Rise and go; your faith has made you {{sozo|well}}!”

units.label: The coming of the kingdom and the day of the Son of Man
units.note: as the lightning flashes, so will the Son of Man be

verses.text: When asked by the Pharisees when the kingdom of God would come, Jesus replied, “The kingdom of God will not come with observable signs.
Nor will people say, ‘Look, here it is,’ or ‘There it is.’ For you see, the kingdom of God is {{entos|in your midst}}.”
Then He said to the disciples, “The time is coming when you will long to see one of the days of the Son of Man, but you will not see it.
People will tell you, ‘Look, there He is!’ or ‘Look, here He is!’ Do not go out or chase after them.
For just as the lightning flashes and lights up the sky from one end to the other, so will be the Son of Man in His day.
But first He must suffer many things and be rejected by this generation.
Just as it was in the days of Noah, so also will it be in the days of the Son of Man:
People were eating and drinking, marrying and being given in marriage, up to the day Noah entered the ark. Then the flood came and destroyed them all.
It was the same in the days of Lot: People were eating and drinking, buying and selling, planting and building.
But on the day Lot left Sodom, fire and sulfur rained down from heaven and destroyed them all.
It will be just like that on the day the Son of Man is revealed.
On that day, let no one on the housetop come down to retrieve his possessions. Likewise, let no one in the field return for anything he has left behind.
Remember Lot’s wife!
Whoever tries to save his life will lose it, but whoever loses his life will preserve it.
I tell you, on that night two people will be in one bed: one will be taken and the other left.
Two women will be grinding grain together: one will be taken and the other left.”
“Where, Lord?” they asked. Jesus answered, “Wherever there is a carcass, there the vultures will gather.”

skandalon.gloss: A stumbling block, a snare, that which trips another into sin. Jesus warns that such things will inevitably come in a fallen world, but woe to the one who causes a little one to stumble. Disciples must watch themselves, for leading the weak into sin is a grave and fearful thing.

pistis.gloss: Faith, trust. The apostles ask for more of it as if it were a quantity, but Jesus answers that faith the size of a mustard seed can uproot a tree. What matters is not the amount but the object: even the smallest true trust, resting on God, is mighty because of the One it holds.

sozo.gloss: To save, to heal, to make whole. Ten were cleansed of leprosy, but only the returning Samaritan hears, your faith has saved you, the word reaching beyond the body to the whole person. Gratitude and faith carry him past healing into salvation, a wholeness the other nine did not receive.

entos.gloss: Within, in the midst of, among. The kingdom of God, Jesus says, is entos of them, already present in His own person and work rather than arriving with a spectacle to be spotted. The King stands among them, so the kingdom is nearer than they think, not out on some future horizon alone.

basic.context: Jesus warns His disciples that things which trip people into sin will come, but woe to the one who causes a little one to stumble; better a millstone and the sea. He commands endless forgiveness of the repentant brother, even seven times a day. When the apostles beg for more faith, He says faith as small as a mustard seed can uproot a tree, and that servants who obey should claim no merit but say, we have only done our duty. On the road ten lepers cry for mercy and are cleansed as they go, but only one, a Samaritan foreigner, returns to fall at Jesus' feet in thanks, and to him alone Jesus says, your faith has made you well. Asked when the kingdom will come, He answers that it is already in their midst, and He warns that the day of the Son of Man will come suddenly like lightning, after He first suffers and is rejected.
basic.hermeneutics: The chapter gathers short teachings on life in the kingdom, forgiveness, humble faith, gratitude, then turns to the kingdom's presence and future. The healing of the ten dramatizes the difference between being cleansed and coming back in worship. The eschatology warns against both false alarms and complacency: the day is certain, sudden, and preceded by the cross.
basic.originalLanguages: Jesus warns of the skandalon, the stumbling block that trips the weak into sin. Faith, pistis, need not be great in quantity, only real, for a seed's worth can move a tree. To the grateful Samaritan He says your faith has sozo you, saved and made you whole, more than the healing the nine received. And the kingdom of God is entos, in their midst, present in the King Himself.
basic.typology: The cleansed leper sent to the priest recalls Leviticus and points to Christ our true cleansing. The days of Noah and Lot become patterns of a world caught unaware by sudden judgment. Lot's wife, looking back, warns against clinging to the doomed old life when God calls us out.
basic.christ: Jesus is the Master whom the lepers call on for mercy and the One at whose feet the Samaritan worships, receiving thanks that belongs to God. He is the Son of Man who must first suffer and be rejected, then be revealed like lightning across the sky. And He is the King in whose presence the kingdom of God has already come into their midst.
basic.god: God is worthy of endless forgiveness passed on to others and of grateful worship returned to Him. He is the God whose kingdom comes not as spectacle but in His Son, and who will one day judge the world suddenly, as in the days of Noah and Lot. He asks humble service that claims no merit and glad thanksgiving that gives Him glory.
basic.crossReferences: Matthew 18 gives the parallel on forgiving the brother. Leviticus 14 sets the law of cleansing the leper. Genesis 19 tells of Lot, Sodom, and his wife. 1 Thessalonians 5 says the day of the Lord comes like a thief, sudden and unexpected.
basic.oneStory: As Jesus nears Jerusalem, He teaches His people to forgive, to trust, and to return in gratitude, and He reveals that the kingdom has already arrived in Him while its consummation waits for the day of the Son of Man. That day will come suddenly, after the cross, so the story presses us to lose our lives now in order to keep them then.

context.literary: The chapter continues Luke's travel narrative with a chain of sayings followed by a healing and a block of teaching on the kingdom's coming. The ten lepers episode echoes the earlier Samaritan themes in Luke, where outsiders often show the faith the insiders lack. The eschatological discourse anticipates the fuller Olivet teaching in Luke 21.
context.historical: Leprosy and related skin diseases brought ceremonial uncleanness and social exile, so the ten kept their distance and had to be certified clean by a priest. Samaritans and Jews were mutually hostile, which sharpens the scandal that only the foreigner gave thanks. The references to Noah's flood and Sodom's fire were vivid, familiar pictures of sudden divine judgment.
context.canonical: The already and not yet of the kingdom, present in Christ yet awaiting His return, runs through the whole New Testament. The days of Noah and Lot as types of the last day appear also in 2 Peter. Losing one's life to save it is a saying Jesus repeats across the Gospels, a hinge of discipleship.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To shape a community marked by mutual forgiveness, humble faith, and grateful worship, and to reveal that the kingdom is present in Jesus now while its sudden consummation, the day of the Son of Man, still lies ahead beyond His suffering and rejection.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The sayings on forgiveness, faith, and duty are directly prescriptive. The healing narrates an event but teaches by example the response of gratitude God seeks. The eschatological sayings describe what will happen and prescribe the readiness, and the letting go, that fits it.

debates.question: What does Jesus mean that the kingdom of God is 'in your midst'?

views.label: Present in the person and work of Jesus among them
views.case: Spoken to hostile Pharisees, 'within you' is unlikely to mean inside their hearts; the natural sense is that the kingdom stands among them in the King Himself, already breaking in through His words and works rather than arriving as a spectacle to be spotted.

views.label: A reality to be received inwardly
views.case: The phrase can be rendered 'within you' or 'within your grasp', stressing that the kingdom is not a matter of external observation but of inward reception and response. On this reading Jesus points them away from sign-watching to the rule of God that must be welcomed personally.

debates.bearing: Either way Jesus redirects them from looking for a future spectacle to reckoning with the King already present, whose reign is to be recognized and received now.

debates.question: What is the 'taken' and 'left' of the day of the Son of Man?

views.label: Taken in judgment, left in safety
views.case: The context is Noah and Lot, where the flood and the fire took the unready away in judgment while the righteous were preserved; 'destroyed them all' frames the taking as removal in judgment, so to be left is to be spared.

views.label: Taken to be with the Lord, left behind for judgment
views.case: Read alongside the gathering of the elect elsewhere, some understand the taking as being gathered to Christ and the leaving as exclusion from His salvation. On this view being taken is the blessing.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree on the urgent point: the day divides humanity suddenly and finally, so the call is to be ready now, holding this life loosely, remembering Lot's wife.

study.originalLanguages: Jesus warns of the skandalon, the stumbling block that trips a little one into sin, a thing so grave that a millstone and the sea would be better than causing it. When the apostles ask for more faith as if it were a measurable quantity, He answers that pistis the size of a mustard seed can uproot a tree, for the power lies not in the amount but in the God it trusts. To the one leper who returns He says your faith has sozo you, a word that means both healed and saved, carrying him past a cleansed body to a whole person, which the silent nine never received. And to the Pharisees He declares the kingdom of God is entos, in their midst, present in the King who stands before them, so that they need not scan the horizon for what has already drawn near.

typology.type: The cleansed leper presented to the priest, Leviticus 14
typology.fulfillment: The ten cleansed by Jesus and sent to the priests, with Christ Himself as the true cleansing, Luke 17:14.

typology.type: The flood in the days of Noah and the fire on Sodom
typology.fulfillment: The sudden judgment of the day when the Son of Man is revealed, Luke 17:26-30.

typology.type: Lot's wife looking back to the doomed city, Genesis 19:26
typology.fulfillment: The warning not to cling to the old life on the day of the Son of Man, Luke 17:31-33.

study.christ: Jesus moves through this chapter as the Master to whom the desperate cry for mercy and the One at whose feet a foreigner worships, receiving the thanksgiving and glory due to God alone. He is the Son of Man who must first suffer many things and be rejected by this generation before His day dawns, so the cross stands between the present kingdom and its glorious consummation. When that day comes it will not be a rumor to chase down back alleys but a flash of lightning across the whole sky, unmistakable and universal. And even now the kingdom is in their midst, because the King is among them; to recognize Him is to receive the reign of God.
study.god: The God of this passage calls His people to a forgiveness as tireless as His own, extended seven times a day to the repentant, and to a humble service that takes no credit but confesses, we have only done our duty. He seeks the worship of grateful hearts, marking the one who returned and asking after the nine who did not. His kingdom comes quietly in His Son before it comes suddenly in power, and He will one day judge the world as surely and as swiftly as He did in the days of Noah and Lot. He is a God to be trusted with the smallest seed of faith and thanked with the loudest voice of praise.

crossReferences.note: Forgive not seven times but seventy times seven, the boundless forgiveness Jesus commands here.
The law for cleansing a leper, the reason Jesus sends the ten to show themselves to the priests.
Lot led out of Sodom and his wife turned to salt for looking back, the warning of Luke 17:32.
The day of the Lord comes like a thief in the night, sudden upon those who feel secure.

study.oneStory: On the road to Jerusalem Jesus forms a people who forgive without limit, trust with mustard-seed faith, and return in gratitude like the Samaritan, and He unveils that the kingdom has already arrived in His own person. Its full and sudden coming, the day of the Son of Man, waits on the far side of His suffering and rejection, so the story runs through the cross toward the lightning-day, urging us to lose our lives now that we may keep them then.

quiz.q: How does Jesus tell His disciples to treat a brother who sins and repents?

options.text: To forgive him, even seven times in a day, whenever he repents.
options.note: Luke 17:3-4 commands repeated, tireless forgiveness of the repentant brother.

options.text: To rebuke him firmly for his sin.
options.note: Rebuke is part of it, but the emphasis falls on forgiving him when he repents.

options.text: To forgive him only if he proves he has changed.
options.note: Jesus ties forgiveness to repentance expressed, not to proof of lasting change.

options.text: To cut him off after repeated offenses.
options.note: The opposite: forgive even seven times in a day.

quiz.q: What does Jesus teach when the apostles ask Him to increase their faith?

options.text: That faith as small as a mustard seed is enough to do the impossible.
options.note: Luke 17:6 shifts the focus from the amount of faith to its reality and object.

options.text: That great faith can uproot trees and move obstacles.
options.note: True of the image, but the point is that even tiny faith suffices, not that faith must be great.

options.text: That they should work to build up a large store of faith.
options.note: Jesus redirects them away from measuring quantity toward small, genuine trust.

options.text: That they do not have enough faith to be His disciples.
options.note: He encourages them that even the smallest true faith is mighty.

quiz.q: What sets the one returning leper apart from the other nine?

options.text: He came back praising God and fell at Jesus' feet in thanks, and to him alone Jesus said his faith had made him well.
options.note: Luke 17:15-19 marks his grateful return and the word of salvation to him.

options.text: He was the only one who was truly healed.
options.note: All ten were cleansed; the difference is that only he returned in gratitude.

options.text: He returned to give thanks, unlike the nine.
options.note: True, but it omits that Jesus pronounced him well, or saved, in a fuller sense.

options.text: He obeyed the command to show himself to the priest.
options.note: All were told to go to the priests; his distinction was coming back to worship.

quiz.q: What does Jesus say about when the kingdom of God comes?

options.text: It does not come with observable signs, for it is already in their midst in Him.
options.note: Luke 17:20-21 locates the kingdom in Jesus' present, not in a spectacle to spot.

options.text: It will not arrive with visible signs to look for.
options.note: True, but it leaves out the positive claim that the kingdom is already present among them.

options.text: It will come at a time no one can ever know.
options.note: The stress here is on its present arrival in Christ, not merely on an unknown timing.

options.text: It will come only when people spot the right signs.
options.note: Jesus says precisely the opposite; it does not come with signs to be observed.

quiz.q: What will the day of the Son of Man be like?

options.text: Sudden and unmistakable like lightning, coming on an unready world as in the days of Noah and Lot.
options.note: Luke 17:24-30 pictures a sudden, universal, unexpected day.

options.text: A day of sudden judgment on those caught unaware.
options.note: True, but it omits the lightning-like visibility and universality of the day.

options.text: A hidden event that only insiders will notice.
options.note: The opposite: it will flash across the whole sky, seen by all.

options.text: Something to be found by chasing reports of 'here' and 'there'.
options.note: Jesus warns them not to chase such reports; the day needs no such search.

questions: Jesus commands forgiveness even seven times a day. Is there someone whose repeated failures have worn out your willingness to forgive, and what would tireless forgiveness look like there?
The apostles wanted more faith, but Jesus pointed to faith like a seed. Are you waiting to feel strong enough to trust God, when He asks only for the small, real faith you already have?
Nine walked away healed; one came back to worship. When God answers your prayers, do you return to give thanks, or move on and forget?
Jesus says the kingdom is already in your midst. Where might you be scanning the horizon for God while missing His nearness in Christ right now?
Remember Lot's wife. What part of your old life are you tempted to keep looking back at, and what would it mean to hold this life loosely for the day of the Son of Man?

translationNotes.note: The printed text sets off 'and he was a Samaritan' with an em dash for emphasis; it is rendered here with a comma. The detail is the sting of the story: the only one who returned to thank Jesus was a foreigner and a religious outsider.

'In your midst' can also be rendered 'within you' or 'within your grasp'. Spoken to hostile Pharisees, it most likely means the kingdom stands among them in the person of Jesus, already present rather than arriving as a spectacle.

The BSB omits verse 36, moving from verse 35 to 37, because the earliest and best manuscripts do not contain it. Some later manuscripts and the Textus Receptus add, 'Two men will be in the field; one will be taken and the other left,' drawn from Matthew 24:40. Its absence changes nothing of the teaching.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Luke 17:1-37. Headings, line breaks, footnote markers, and speaker labels were skipped when assembling verse text. The em dash in verse 16 was converted to a comma. Verse 36 is absent from the BSB, following the earliest manuscripts, and is carried in the translationNotes. Sermon status set to none; no sermon from the trusted cluster was verified this session.
